overview: Stanwich is northern Greenwich's premier estate enclave, centered on Stanwich Road and North Street in the town's back country zone. The Stanwich Club, whose Tom Fazio-redesigned course Golf Digest has ranked number one in Connecticut, anchors the social and recreational life of the area. Multi-acre properties here set behind long driveways define a landscape built for privacy at scale.

children.text: There is no sign marking where Stanwich begins. No commercial district, no village center, no sidewalk. What you notice instead is space. The road narrows, stone walls appear on both sides, and the houses — when you can see them at all — sit far back from the pavement behind hedgerows, specimen trees, and iron gates. This is estate-zoned Greenwich. Two-acre minimums. Four-acre parcels that are common. Occasional ten-acre compounds that function as private worlds.

The Stanwich Club is the neighborhood's social nucleus. Originally founded in 1961, the club underwent a comprehensive redesign of its golf course by Tom Fazio, and the result has been ranked the number-one course in Connecticut by Golf Digest. Membership is highly selective. Beyond golf, the club offers tennis, paddle tennis, swimming, and dining in a setting that preserves the back country's aesthetic: understated, members-only, no spectacle. For the families who live along Stanwich Road and the intersecting lanes that radiate outward, the club is where community happens in a neighborhood that has no downtown.

Architecturally, Stanwich is traditional Greenwich at its most refined. Center-hall colonials with white clapboard and black shutters. Shingle-style estates with wrap-around porches and multiple wings. Stone manors that reference English country houses. The homes are large — 5,000 to 12,000 square feet is the range — but they tend to sit within the landscape rather than dominate it. Mature plantings, rolling lawns, and the absence of visible neighbors create an atmosphere that Manhattan transplants describe as the opposite of everything they left behind.

Living in Stanwich requires a deliberate trade. You gain acreage, silence, and a level of privacy that southern Greenwich cannot provide. You give up walkability. The nearest Metro-North station is a fifteen-minute drive. Groceries, restaurants, and schools all require a car. Residents here have made that calculation and chosen land over convenience, and the prices — ranging from $3 million to well above $15 million depending on acreage and improvements — reflect the scarcity of what Stanwich offers.

highlights: Stanwich Club features a Tom Fazio-redesigned golf course ranked number one in Connecticut by Golf Digest
Estate-zoned parcels of two to ten acres with long driveways, stone walls, and mature specimen plantings define the streetscape
Properties range from $3 million to above $15 million depending on acreage, improvements, and proximity to the club
Traditional architecture — center-hall colonials, shingle-style estates, and stone manors — spans 5,000 to 12,000 square feet
housingCharacteristics:
Stanwich's housing stock is uniformly high-end, built across several decades but united by a traditional Greenwich aesthetic. Center-hall colonials predominate, many constructed or substantially renovated in the 1990s and 2000s with modern systems behind period-appropriate exteriors. Shingle-style homes with cedar siding and multi-gable rooflines are well represented, alongside stone-and-stucco properties that draw on English and French country influences. Square footage typically ranges from 5,000 to 12,000 square feet, with guest houses, pool complexes, and equestrian facilities appearing on the larger parcels.

Lot sizes are the defining variable. Two-acre zoning is the minimum, but many properties encompass four, six, or even ten acres of rolling terrain with mature hardwood canopy. Driveways are long and often gated. The visual result is a neighborhood where homes are screened from the road and from each other, creating a level of seclusion that has attracted hedge fund principals, corporate executives, and private families for decades.
marketNotes:
Stanwich trades at the upper tier of the Greenwich market. Asking prices range from roughly $3 million for smaller parcels with older homes requiring renovation to $15 million or more for turnkey estates on five-plus acres with modern amenities. The buyer pool is narrow by design — these are discretionary purchasers who have already evaluated Manhattan, Westchester, and other Fairfield County options before committing to the back country lifestyle.

Inventory moves slowly compared to the village neighborhoods. Marketing periods of three to six months are typical for properties above $8 million. Private, off-market transactions account for a meaningful share of sales in this tier. Sellers benefit from patience, precise pricing, and broker networks that extend to New York City wealth management circles. Overpricing is penalized more severely here than in faster-moving neighborhoods because comparable sales data is sparse and buyers are methodical.
locationAccess:
Stanwich is a car-dependent neighborhood situated along Stanwich Road and North Street in northern Greenwich. The Merritt Parkway (Route 15) is accessible within minutes, connecting to Stamford, Norwalk, and Westchester County. The closest Metro-North stations are Greenwich and Cos Cob, each approximately fifteen minutes by car, with express service to Grand Central Terminal in 47 to 55 minutes.

Greenwich Avenue is a fifteen-minute drive south for dining, shopping, and cultural institutions including the Bruce Museum. The Whole Foods on East Putnam Avenue and the Stop & Shop in Cos Cob serve as primary grocery destinations. Despite the drive times, residents of Stanwich accept the commute as the cost of acreage — the privacy, the land, and the absence of traffic noise are precisely what they purchased.
seoTitle: Stanwich, Greenwich CT Real Estate & Estate Homes
seoDescription: Explore Stanwich in Greenwich, CT — multi-acre estates near Stanwich Club's top-ranked golf course. View properties and call Matt Caiola for a showing.

faqs.question: What is the Stanwich Club and why does it matter for real estate?
faqs.answer: The Stanwich Club is a private country club on Stanwich Road whose Tom Fazio-redesigned golf course has been ranked number one in Connecticut by Golf Digest. Membership is highly selective and includes golf, tennis, paddle tennis, swimming, and dining. Proximity to the club is a significant value driver for estate properties in the neighborhood, as it serves as the social and recreational center for an area that has no commercial district.

faqs.question: What do homes cost in Stanwich, Greenwich CT?
faqs.answer: Stanwich properties typically range from approximately $3 million for smaller parcels with homes requiring renovation to above $15 million for turnkey estates on five or more acres. Pricing is driven primarily by acreage, the quality of improvements, and proximity to the Stanwich Club. The narrow buyer pool and sparse comparable sales data mean that accurate pricing at the time of listing is especially critical in this micro-market.

faqs.question: How far is Stanwich from the train station and major highways?
faqs.answer: The Greenwich and Cos Cob Metro-North stations are each approximately fifteen minutes by car from Stanwich, offering express service to Grand Central Terminal in 47 to 55 minutes. The Merritt Parkway (Route 15) is accessible within minutes for highway travel north and east. Stanwich is a car-dependent neighborhood, and residents should plan on driving for all commuting, shopping, and school transportation.

faqs.question: What types of homes are in the Stanwich area?
faqs.answer: Stanwich features predominantly traditional architecture including center-hall colonials, shingle-style estates, and stone-and-stucco manor homes ranging from roughly 5,000 to 12,000 square feet. Properties sit on two-to-ten-acre parcels with long driveways, mature landscaping, and extensive privacy screening. Many larger estates include guest houses, pool complexes, tennis courts, and equestrian facilities on the grounds.
